desc:
A big anvil dominates the centre of the room, while along the east wall stands a
melting furnace which is fed by a pair of bellows. The furnace gives an orange
gleam to the room. A variety of tools are hanging on the walls such as pinchers
and fliers. In a corner some metal scrap is piled. A dented pail full with dark,
oily water stands between the pile and the anvil. Both the walls and the roof
have been turned dark with grime.
name: Blacksmiths

desc:
This square inside the small village has a well in its centre that has been made
from stones of various sizes. Several posts support a small roof a few feet
above the top of the well. A simple winch, with an attached rope and dented
pail, is mounted on the well's posts. To the west stands the main building of
the village. To the north there is a forge, and a gate east of here leads to a
wooded pasture. A tall watchtower standing over the village square can be
reached by the wooden ladder here.
name: Inside a Small Village

desc:
The ground here appears quite trampled for a pasture field, not many plants
remaining for the animals to eat. The areas around the gate to the west and the
barn on the north side are especially bad due to all the animals herded through
on a regular schedule. Perhaps one day if left to prosper the village will
expand this pasture. Close to the gate there are two troughs, one for water and
one for fodder. In the southeast corner there is a steaming dunghill.
name: Wooded Pasture

desc:
The walls of the barn are made of sturdy wooden planks and the roof is thatched.
Along the walls are wide stalls for the animals. The barn was built with a
hayloft and it is now full of hay. Standing from a crate just inside the
entrance are several pitchforks, spades and a few other tools. A wooden
wheelbarrow has been left carefully out of the way beside the crate. South of
the barn stretches a wooded pasture.
name: Inside a Barn

desc:
This is the sleeping chamber of the men of the village. The northern and eastern
walls of the room are lined with beds. A small table and a chair stands along
the west wall. The beds are covered with well made quilts and lush furs.
name: Sleeping Chambers

desc:
This is the main hall of the settlement; its walls are made from huge logs. A
long wooden table, surrounded by chairs, takes up the majority of the space in
this hall. In the western wall there is a huge stove built of thick irregular
stones. The northern wall of the room is covered by some curtains.
name: Main Hall

desc:
This small watchtower has been placed above the gates in the palisade. It has a
thatched roof to protect the watchmen from sun and rain and its walls are made
of thick planks. From up here it is possible to watch much of the surrounding
land and easily keep track of people moving around.
name: In a Watchtower

desc:
The fertile soil of these plains give the lush, green grass its life and
abundance. The plains continue to the north and east, it appears without end.
However, several miles to the west, one could, if very observant, see the
treetops of a small grove of trees and perhaps note the small village that lies
just beyond.
name: Vast Grasslands

desc:
The wild grass so prominent in these lands, is untainted by tree or bush for
miles to the east and north. A vast sea of grass, as green as the ocean is blue,
overwhelms the senses by its very size. In contrast, to the west lies a small
man-made elm grove. And although it is obscured from view, even the most inept
traveller can make out the signs of a large settlement just beyond the trees.
name: Edge of the Vast Grasslands

desc:
Running from the southeast to the northwest, rows of young elm trees extend in
perfect lines. Though not yet mature, the branches of these trees are wide
enough to block most of the light from the sky, offering travellers a nice bit
of shade or a cosy spot to camp. Just to the west, a large settlement obscures
the horizon.
name: Grove of Trees

desc:
Vast, green grasslands extend from here to the north and east for as far as the
eye can see. These plains are home to many animals but dominated by horses.
These wild, yet friendly animals can be seen throughout these grass-covered
lands. Occasionally these noble creatures will bear a weary traveller on his or
her way.
name: Edge of the Grasslands

desc:
Surrounded on all sides by the vast green land of the Anduin plains, a lone
traveller would seem like a small ship adrift on the Great Sea. These plains, so
beautiful and majestic to some, could become barren and life-threatening to
others. These plains give no food and no water, only grass for farther to the
east than mortal eyes can see.
name: Vast Grassland

desc:
The plains continue to the east, with no end in sight. In every direction, a sea
of green grass blankets the landscape. There is no sign of shelter or water for
several miles. Off in the distant west, the ground rises, forming low hills.
Occassionally, a wisp of smoke crosses the western sky.
name: Vast Grasslands

desc:
The plains continue to the east, further than the eye can see. The grass
stretches out for miles in every direction, offering no landscape to guide by;
only your track heading off into the distance. However, soon the grass swallows
even your tracks, repairing the perfect sea of green.
name: Vast Grasslands

desc:
The grass-covered plains stretch out for miles in every direction. The green
grass blankets the land, turning the earth into a sea of green seemingly as vast
as the sea of blue. Far in the west, even on the murkiest day, the shapes of
large hills and levees form a line in the horizon.
name: Vast Grassland

desc:
The grassy plains continue in every direction, a pure sea of green,
uncontaminated by tree or bush. In the east however, the Grasslands seem to go
on and on, farther than mortal eyes can see, seemingly forever. A few miles to
the south, a small homestead can be seen lying just before a large village. To
the east of the village, treetops offer an excellent feature for travellers to
guide by for many miles.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
Moving through the Grasslands, it becomes apparent that large numbers of horses
must graze in and wander these plains. Occasionally, large patches of grass will
be eaten away and in their place, many hoof prints dot the earth. The appeal of
this land for creatures that live off vegetation is clear. As long as the
weather allows, the grass in these plains seems to continue forever.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
Everywhere there is grass and nothing more. As full of life as these fertile
plains are, to a traveller without food or water, these plains would be as
plentiful as dunes in a desert. For as far as mortal eyes can see in the east,
the Grasslands continue onwards, without end. The more observant, experienced
wanderer might catch a glimpse of the tops of a grove of trees, far in the south
or even catch sight of smoke coming from a village just west of that grove.
name: Drowning in the Grasslands

desc:
In the east, there is grass. In the west: grass. In the south: grass. In the
north: even more grass. It hugs the land for miles in every direction. It offers
no guidance. It gives travellers nothing to steer their course by. It goes on,
seemingly without end, like a sea, a sea of green.
name: Green Plains

desc:
In this sea of green, there is little to catch the eye. In every direction, the
grass flows like the waves on the ocean, small ripples that seem to go on
forever. Occasionally, one might catch sight of a herd of horses, roaming and
grazing in the Grasslands. The plains in the east continue for farther than the
mortal eyes can see so finding these animals is a rare find indeed.
name: Green Plains

desc:
Grass blankets the land in a seemingly unending sea of green. In the east, the
tranquility of the grass threatens to draw travellers into the forever, into the
beyond. Far in the west, what appear to be hills or levees form a line running
north and south, as if they were constructed to block something in or out. But
from this vantage, so far away, the purpose of those rises in the plains will
remain a mystery.
name: Grasslands

desc:
Moving along the western edge of the Grasslands, travellers in this region might
note the line of levees and hills in the west that appear to form a retaining
wall. However, from this elevation, it is not possible to see what lies beyond
the natural and man-made hills. Several miles in the south, a small grove of
trees can be seen on a clear day, while in the east, the Grasslands seem to
continue forever.
name: Grasslands

desc:
Natives of this land call the massive, green plains in the south The Grasslands.
There really isn't a better way to describe the immense field of nothing but
green, thick grass for more miles than mortal eyes can see. The Grasslands seem
as peaceful and serene as a calm lake, but the ill-prepared will soon find that
as rich and fertile as the soil is for the grass, the land offers little for
creatures that subsist on more than grass. No bush, tree or water source is
visible for miles.
name: Grasslands

desc:
The Grasslands end abruptly here, as a small group of merchants have taken it
upon themselves to start a settlement here. Passage in the north is blocked by a
large fence with no gate on its southern side. The only way to continue
northwards is to go around to the east or west. In the south, the Grasslands
continue for many more miles than mortal eyes can see.
name: Grasslands

desc:
At the northern edge of a large grassy plain named the Grasslands by locals, a
vast expanse of flat, green grass flows out to the southeast, apparently without
end. Heading south would be very much like starting an ocean voyage. Without the
proper planning and supplies, hunger and thirst would leave the unprepared
traveller adrift in a sea of green, praying for rescue.
name: Grasslands

desc:
An adventurous farmer must own these lands, for a crop grows here that is rarely
seen and never this far north: cotton. Healthy cotton plants are the only thing
growing in this field and in the fall, if the weather permits, these plants will
produce a plentiful bounty. Just beyond the field to the south, a vast sea of
green grass engulfs the land.
name: Cultivated Fields

desc:
From atop this steep incline, any creature interested in watching the habits of
Beorning merchants would be happy indeed to find this vantage point. From this
elevation, one can clearly watch the comings and goings of the inhabitants of
this tiny hamlet. A bee farmer has set up shop in the southern end of the
Village and his hives sit just behind his shop. An animal trainer works with his
horses in a paddock at the north end of town. Between the two, a run-down shack
threatens to collapse on any who enter it.
name: Hill above a Village

desc:
A small footpath widens as it approaches a small settlement to the east. There
are a multitude of horse tracks leading to and from this tiny village along the
path. Directly south, a large hill borders the road and may offer an excellent
view of the surrounding lands. The strong, sweet scent of honey gets stronger in
the direction of the village.
name: Worn Path

desc:
This path runs to the east and south, following the base of a large hill. Grass
taller than a man in the west and hills in the south block the horizon in those
directions. In the north, a seemingly perfect sea of grass envelops the land.
Under the protective shadow of a large hill in the east, a small settlement can
be seen.
name: Bend in a Worn Path

desc:
At the base of a large hill to the east and a smaller one to the south, a path
has been worn by the footfalls of man and beast. While the path crosses over the
southern hill, it curves around the eastern one because going over that rise
would be difficult, as steep as it is. Just off the path, to the west, a small
campsite is nestled between hills to the south and west of it.
name: On a Worn Path

desc:
The path is a bit wilder here than in the south, perhaps because the hill comes
to a small plateau here before rising to the west and northeast. This plateau
offers an excellent spot to take in the lay of the land before continuing
onwards. To the north, this path becomes more pronounced as it winds to the east
around a large hill.
name: Path over a Grassy Hill

desc:
This small but distinct path continues running to the north and south, bisecting
the grass-covered plains to the east and west. In the north, the plains give way
to small hills, also covered by the grass that envelopes the land in every
direction. This path crosses the hills in the north and seems to continue past
them.
name: Path in the Grass

desc:
Beneath a levee just to the west, a small, well-worn path continues to the north
and south. To the east, vast grassy plains continue further than any mortal eye
can see. This path is very easy to follow and must be travelled often to be as
distinct as it is against the lush, green grass.
name: Footpath

desc:
A small footpath has been worn through the grass-covered lands, running north
and west. Such a small path would have been overrun by the grass if it was not
used regularly. Such trails usually mark trade routes between two villages.
Perhaps the large village to the south has a sister village further north.
name: Footpath

desc:
Weaving and winding its way around the levees and past smaller obstacles, this
small foot path serves as a marker for travellers and traders from the large
Beorning settlement in the south and the smaller one in the north. The path
wildly wanders through the green grass in the fashion of paths only made by the
footfalls of two-legged creatures. No animal tracks can be seen on this small
walkway.
name: Faint Winding Path

desc:
The path eventually heads north-east, but in an indirect way. At times, the
trail turns away from its intended direction, avoiding a rock here, a hole
there. Despite its meandering, the path remains clear and easy to follow.
Countless footfalls have worn away the vegetation and even in darkness it would
be simple to keep on the trail.
name: Winding Faint Path

desc:
This obviously man-made levee serves a vital role in keeping the flood waters in
the west at bay. If not for this steep, abrupt rise in the plains, the waters
that sit stagnant would flow towards the plains in the south, washing away the
Old Forest Road and the Beorning settlement directly south of here.
name: Atop the Mud Plains Levee

desc:
At the base of this massive elm tree, it is almost impossible to see the
watchtower directly overhead. Although it is the topmost part of a man-made
structure, it uses the elm as its main support and uses the leaves as its
camouflage. A careless traveller, attempting to sneak up on this village, would
be in for a nasty surprise when passing this lookout point.
name: Under the Watchtower

desc:
The walls surrounding this settlement appear solid from a distance. But once
closer, the true nature of this brush wall appears. Mature elm trees have been
used as supports for an intricately woven wall of vines and brush. The skilled
architects of this defence have constructed this wall so densely that no light
from the inside village escapes.
name: Outside the Village Walls

desc:
This unusually small plantation doesn't appear to produce any crops. Instead,
the owner of this land has rows of beehives on one side of the path leading to
the house and cows and goats grazing on the other. Several farmhands tend to the
milling of the animals while others harvest the honey and wax from the hives.
The master of this plantation however, is nowhere to be seen.
name: Small Plantation

desc:
This simple abode exemplifies the typical Beorning home. Simplistic yet
functional, nothing in this home is without its proper place. Nothing is too
fancy but these people still manage to keep some of the finer things in life. A
simple set of dishes is displayed on a shelf next to a polished set of metal
utensils. In the oven, the smell of a pork-pie wafts onto the plantation.
name: Humble Home

desc:
The fortified brush walls of this village have a distinctly different feel on
the inside than they do on the exterior. What looks like an impenetrable wall of
live wood and thorns from the outside takes on a total change of persona within.
Flowers stick their heads out from the loosely intertwined vines which
themselves are covered in moss and leaves. A green wall that thrives with life
and vitality also protects the lives of the inhabitants of this village.
name: Inside the Settlement Walls

desc:
Hanging from the ceiling of this Spartan room is its only decoration, a large
brass oil lamp. Even during the day, this lamp stays lit as a reminder to all
who come here that like that lamp, the people of this small hamlet are always
prepared for whatever darkness may fall.
name: Guardroom

desc:
Climbing through the leaves and branches of this elm tree brings you to the
northern watchtower of Ingrove. From this camouflaged vantage, guards can spot
travellers to this area, miles before they come close enough to the city to be
of any threat. Those standing guard on this tower do not fear attack from
raiders, for even wood elves would have a difficult time spotting anyone through
the watchtower's thick cover of leaves and branches.
name: Watchtower

desc:
Underfoot, a carefully constructed stone road paves the main thoroughfare of
Ingrove. Unlike most stone roads which use large, flat rocks, this road is
composed of smaller, round stones. They have been driven into the underlying
dirt with such force that the heaviest foot traffic could not disturb them.
Small, flat coloured rocks have been embedded between the larger stones giving
this road an almost smooth appearance. Directly north and south, the sounds of
haggling merchants can be heard.
name: Bear's Way

desc:
Standing in the centre of this modest home, a ten foot tall shining bronze
sculpture scrapes the ceiling. Free form in its design and composition, this
massive collage of swords, shields and armour has been forged into the shape of
a massive bear, rearing up on its hind legs as if preparing to strike down
anyone who comes here. After the initial shock of seeing a bear made of swords
fades, one might notice the obsessively neat and ordered collection of small
arms and light armour that hangs from perfect rows of hooks on the wall.
name: Blacksmith's Home

desc:
On every bit of counter or cabinet or floor space in this home, some collection
of goods sits covered in a light film of dust and what appears to be flour. Odds
are, a traveller seeking goods for a journey would do well to inquire here. A
small table by the wooden front door has dozens of tiny flasks resting upon it.
Above and around the door frame, lanterns of every shape and size hang ready for
use. Blankets hang from the walls like tapestries with racks of dried meat
sitting nearby. Dozens of footprints have been left on the floor in the thick
coating of white dust. A large door in the eastern wall leads into another room
of the house.
name: In the Home of a Rotund Couple

desc:
The light coating of flour that sits in this couple's main room is multiplied
one hundred fold in their kitchen. It is impossible to move without stirring up
a cloud of flour. Every item in this room is a strange shade of white, from the
floor to the clay jars that sit on the wide counters. A large stove, built into
the southern wall, stays lit day and night. The smell of baking bread fills the
room.
name: Rotund Couple's Kitchen

desc:
A varied assortment of items line the shelves, haphazardly nailed to the wall.
At first glance, the shelves appear to be full of mindless junk. But on closer
inspection, it becomes clear that the items stored here are rare finds indeed.
Old pieces of wall from the decimated city of Old Fornost, a scrap of wood from
the original sign at the Prancing Pony Inn and a chunk of raw ore from the Blue
Mountains are just a few of the items the owner of this home has collected. Most
Beornings are wary of wandering too far from home but the resident of this abode
must be an exception to the rule.
name: Traveller's Hut

desc:
This town's north-south thoroughfare ends here. Directly to the south, a massive
wall of plant life stands as the village's protection. Small homes in the east
and west contain the road, which heads north. The road stands in the shade of
the wall and the homes most of the day. At night, small lanterns reflect light
off the foliage, bathing this cove in a green glow.
name: Homestead Road

desc:
This is a tiny house. As soon as leaving the doorway, the dining table blocks
the path. Moving around that, a clothes line threatens to decapitate anyone
taller than a hobbit. Once clear of those obstacles, a huge, brown stove takes
up most of the room. There doesn't appear to be any room for a family to live
here. But the clothes on the line clearly belong to children as well as adults.
name: Simple Abode

desc:
The road narrows in the south and opens more to the north. Dominating the view
in the north is the shape of a massive, unmoving bear. The sound of running
water can also be heard in the north and in the east. But the most overwhelming
assault on the senses comes from the west, where the smell of baking bread flows
from the kitchen of a large home.
name: Homestead Road

desc:
A small, well-shaded area has been left undeveloped by the residents of this
town. Small benches have been erected in this small park to give passers-by a
dry, comfortable place to sit. Several mature elms give much needed shade from
the sun and green, lush grass softens the earth underfoot.
name: Shady Park

desc:
These stables are arguably the cleanest ones ever seen. The light smell of hay
is the only indicator that horses are even kept here. Saddles hang in neat and
orderly rows on the southern wall, each with its own riding whip coiled around
the saddle horn. Overhead, hanging from hooks in the rafters, many clean buckets
with brushes sticking out over the rims wait for their next use. A small wooden
sign hangs above the stable doors.
name: Fine Stables

desc:
This is the main room of a large, stately house. Many family portraits hang from
the walls and plush couches and chairs are placed in every conceivable location.
Thick, vibrant curtains hang from every window sill and large tapestries cover
the walls. Above the fireplace, a life size portrait of a regal woman dominates
the room.
name: Living Room of a Large Home

desc:
At the top of a large staircase, many doors line a long, mirrored hallway. Each
of these guest rooms has been offered to travellers and those who have no home.
Each door in the hallway has been painted a different colour, perhaps to ease
guests in identifying which rooms are theirs.
name: In a Mirrored Hallway

desc:
The earth is dry here, worn barren by the repeated footfalls of those
individuals coming here for practice sessions. An experienced scout uses this
yard as her training grounds for young Beornings she trains to be village
guardians and someday, warriors. In the centre of this yard, a large training
ring has been constructed. Along the northern wall a range for archery practice
is fenced with poles every few yards as warning markers.
name: Training Yard

desc:
This is the home of a warrior, without a doubt. Hanging over the mantle are two
crossed swords, both worn dull from repeated use in battle. Framing the swords
on either side are two bronze shields, equally battered. Other than those items,
the walls are bare. A small cot and a table with two chairs are the only
furniture in the room. Out back, swords can be heard clanging against each
other.
name: Home of a Hardened Warrior

desc:
The overwhelming stench of rotten food and mildew slams you in the face. How
anyone could live in this overbearing smell of dirt and rot confounds the
imagination. On a table, the only piece of furniture that any creature from this
century could recognise, sits a totally green piece of bread. Rats scurry about
underfoot and spiders spin their webs overhead.
name: Hag's House

desc:
The stone-paved road comes to an end here as it reaches the northern wall of the
village. On either side of the road the fronts of two small homes face the
street. Overhead, the tops of mature elm trees provide shade for this quiet row.
In the south, the busy hustle and bustle of the town can be heard.
name: Homestead Road

desc:
Small and quaint, this cosy abode homes a happy couple who have recently become
parents. Baby clothes and blankets are flung over the furniture and on the
floor. Hanging in the back of the house are diapers, drying from a recent
washing. But despite the mess created by the newborn and its less than prepared
parents, this home still has a warm and welcoming feel.
name: Cosy Home

desc:
The leader of this Beorning settlement oversees the village concerns from this
humble abode. A small window with tattered curtains looks out onto the street.
Upon large hooks bolted into the ceiling, several axes of various shapes and
sizes hang ready to be used at a moment's notice. The east wall has a crude
opening cut into it. Behind the opening, the gate of a massive metal cage can be
seen. The cage appears to have been crafted to hold large animals, as the bars
are extremely thick and spaced wide apart.
name: Chieftain's Home

desc:
This well-worn bit of road is the busiest in the village. Training grounds for
the village guardians lie just to the east. A small, ill-kept house in the west
has a constant stream of men entering and exiting with much haste. Traffic seems
lighter in the south, though much noisier.
name: Homestead Road

desc:
This is the central meeting place for Beornings living in these plains. A large
fountain, sculpted from granite in the shape of a black bear, is the main source
of water for this village, being constructed atop a fresh spring. To the east,
the road runs up to the front door of a large house. Northwest, a large, iron
cage sits next to a small, unimpressive hut. The cage appears to be covered in
some sort of red, sticky goo and a few barrels stand near it, filled with rotten
fruit of various kinds.
name: Watering Hole

desc:
The main thoroughfare of this village runs in a straight, east to west, line. It
narrows in the east, leading to a large house and quickly widens westward,
leading to the town square. Small flowers have been planted on both roadsides.
The rows of flowers lead to the front door of the spacious home in the east.
Just off the road in the south, a clump of trees offer shade to a secluded park.
name: Bear's Way

desc:
A stone road leads to a huge stone fountain in the east and a wall of green a
bit farther west. Utilitarian in nature, this road also serves an aesthetic
purpose. Using the stones as an artistic medium, the builder has crafted the
image of a massive black bear in the road. The flat sculpture is so large that
it would not be visible to those standing directly above it but would appear to
be a dark patch in the stones. But viewed from the outer edges, a larger than
life bear can be seen, looking towards the west with its head turned slightly
upwards.
name: Bear's Way

desc:
This square, metal cage sits adjacent to a small house. It measures about ten
feet by ten feet and is capable of containing several men at a time, as its
former use was as a trap for large game, bears in particular. Now, the
authorities of this village have decided to put it to a more practical use, a
holding cell for criminals. The lock on the side of the cage is a very strong
and clever device. But even if it could be picked, the guards waiting on the
other side of the bars would not take kindly to escaping criminals.
name: Bear Trap

desc:
This small village is made up of only three buildings, constructed with their
front doors facing in a semicircle to the west. Few would even refer to this
collection of structures as a village if it were not for the large sign standing
before you, proclaiming such. An odd mixture of smells and sounds fill the air
here.
name: Inside Honey Village

desc:
A small bed, a table and three chairs are the only furniture in this small home,
however, every bit of wall space has been taken by shelves. It almost looks like
the house has been built out of shelving. Sitting on the shelves, causing them
to bow severely are jars and jars of honey. Every flat surface in the house is
used to harvest honey out of its combs. With so much honey about, the occasional
bee, buzzing around your head, is to be expected.
name: Bee-keeper's Abode

desc:
Animal furs of all kinds line the walls of this large shack. Wolf and bear traps
hang, uncleaned from last use, from hooks in the wall. A squarish hole in the
wall serves as the only window. The door hangs haphazardly on its hinges but the
lock is of the finest quality. This shack's owner does not seem to place much
interest in appearances but pays a great deal of attention to security.
name: Trapper's Lodge

desc:
The keeper of this farm has fenced in the land in the same way a horse breeder
would fence in his paddock. However, there are no animals here, only insects and
thousands of them. The large fences seem to be to keep animals out, rather than
keeping anything trapped in. Within the fences are many rows of beehives,
running from north to south. A few sit unused, but the vast majority are
literally humming with working bees, producing honey and wax. The hives are
constructed of wood so they can be easily opened by their owner.
name: Bee Farm

desc:
Here under the floor of a run-down shack, a hunter has stashed a variety of
trophies. Large bear furs, tanned deer skins and used animal traps cover the
walls and floor. It is highly unusual for anyone to hunt bears in this part of
Arda so it is clear why the owner of this shack hides the spoils of his hunts.
name: Trapper's Stash

desc:
The earth softly rises and falls underfoot as the plains attempt to reclaim the
land taken by the hills long ago. From a distance however, this land would
appear as flat as any plain to the casual observer, as the grass that grows here
is tall enough to obscure the real lay of the land from view. Several miles to
the south, a large hill gives travellers a landmark to guide by. Just east of
that hill, a small settlement has grown up in the hill's shadow.
name: Swallowed by the Grass

desc:
The plant-life in this field has been eaten away by grazing animals, looking for
a snack. Domestic animals are kept in the small settlement south of here and on
any given day, several of the more tame and obedient animals are let out of the
corral to graze. Many wander in this patchy, grass-covered area, nibbling at the
grass and looking for treats from passers-by.
name: Grazed Field

desc:
Adjacent to a fenced in pasture in the south, this piece of land is not owned by
anyone, so domesticated animals have made it a grazing pasture. The grass only
grows in patches that dwindle farther south. In the north, a sea of grass
stretches for more miles than mortal eyes can see.
name: Grazing Land

desc:
The overwhelming stench filling the air leaves little doubt that animals graze
nearby. The grass only grows in patches in this corral as domesticated animals
are kept here and the soil suffers the effects. Horses and cattle mill about
near the west and south gates, waiting for the occasional treat from their
owners or passers-by.
name: Pasture

desc:
In this messy tangle of thick, man-high stems, progress becomes troublesome,
because of both the depth of the grass and its sharp, scratching edges. At
times, you have trouble getting your bearings, as, no matter where you look,
it's only grass all around, and there are no references to guide by. And
sometimes, it even seems as if you were circling around. But fortunately, the
sky is always above your head, and perhaps the sun or the stars may help.
name: Deep inside the Grass

desc:
A rocky hill extends in this area. At the feet of its northern and eastern
hillsides, the ground seems to have subsided into a deep hollow, the very steep
slopes descending many yards downwards to the bottom. But near the top, a stone
promontory extends out towards the sky, as if hanging on the air, and the sights
there are impressive. A great expanse lies before you, covered in deep-green
grass. And such grass! The stalks rise so high that they could easily conceal a
standing man, and they swing in the wind forming waves that move up and down the
plain, so that, from the viewpoint, it looks like a great green sea.
name: A Natural Viewpoint over a Sea of Grass

desc:
Grass grows especially thick in this area, the tangled stems severely hindering
your advance. Deep silence and intense stillness mysteriously surround this
dense forest of grass stalks, as if no sound from outside, not even the howl of
the wind, was able to come into the depths of this place, and no thing nor being
dared to disturb its deep lethargy. Light is dim beneath the enclosing stem
ceiling, and, in the half-darkness, the puzzling halo strengthens.
name: A Still and Quiet Area

desc:
Half-hidden among the high surrounding stalks, a clearer area suddenly comes
into sight: not like a clearing, as grass still grows thick, but undoubtedly
less dense. And this place seems to have a special meaning for the great red
deer, as, during rut season, the large bucks come here to duel, their long
antlers clashing in bloody fights to death. Strangely enough, there are no bones
lying among the grass, nor any other signs of the defeated deer.
name: The Male Deer Duelling Place

desc:
In this messy tangle of thick, man-high stems, progress becomes troublesome,
because of both the depth of the grass and its sharp, scratching edges. At
times, you have trouble getting your bearings, as, no matter where you look,
it's only grass all around, and there are no references to guide by. And
sometimes, it even seems as if you were circling around. But fortunately, the
sky is always above your head, and perhaps the sun or the stars may help.
name: Deep inside the Grass

desc:
In this messy tangle of thick, man-high stems, progress becomes troublesome,
because of both the depth of the grass and its sharp, scratching edges. At
times, you have trouble getting your bearings, as, no matter where you look,
it's only grass all around, and there are no references to guide by. And
sometimes, it even seems as if you were circling around. But fortunately, the
sky is always above your head, and perhaps the sun or the stars may help.
name: Deep inside the Grass

desc:
A group of small hillocks with rounded tops rise slightly over the ground. Grass
grows abundant on their hillsides, and only occasionally a smooth-edged stone
shows among the stalks, as if forgotten long ago. Perhaps one day the proud tops
of these hills towered above the grasslands, but nowadays they have become no
more than a small unevenness of the ground, as if time and weather had slowly
buried them in the earth, thus pushing them back to their source rock.
name: Old, Eroded Hills

desc:
The monotony of the plains breaks suddenly, as the grasslands run into a deep
depression. To the east, the ground seems to have fallen down, forming a small
cliff that rises over the bottom, and its bare, earthy wall stands almost
upright, exposing the naked soil to the light. From the edge of the sheer drop,
the ground looks a long way down, and covered by a great expanse where grass
grows outstandingly high, as if trying to climb up to the top. The deep lowland
spreads in the distance, but it rises slowly northwards till it levels up the
plains.
name: Top of an Embankment

desc:
Time has turned these once mighty hills into little more than grass-covered
anthills. Telltale pebbles, victims of time's harsh treatment, litter the
landscape, giving travellers on foot something to kick up as they cross the
hill. In the south, a small foot path takes a turn eastwards towards a small
settlement. Farther south, the land rises as larger hills take prominence over
the plains.
name: Hills Worn Smooth

desc:
A small hill grows westward, completing a circle of hills in the west. By far
the smallest of the ring of hills, this grassy rise plays its role, just as well
as its larger neighbours. In the west and down the slope, a small glen thrives
inside the protective stance of the ring. Down the eastern slope, a foot path
has formed where traders and travellers have worn the grass away.
name: Grassy Rise

desc:
As the land flattens out in the east, hills rise up in the west obscuring the
lay of the western land from view. Grass covers the land in every direction,
turning the rises and drops of the landscape into a rolling sea of green. Just a
bit farther south, a large levee has been constructed to contain the flooding
waters that cover the western plains.
name: Grasslands

desc:
Just west of a worn foot path, travellers have used this spot so regularly to
rest it resembles a permanent campsite. The grass has been worn flat, yet still
grows. A small ring of stones marks the usual spot for the campfire. Poking
sticks, used to keep a fire alive, lie strewn on the ground, in the hopes of
future use. And two large logs, cut from mature elms, offer a place to rest
weary legs, or lean against during a well-deserved sleep.
name: Small Campsite

desc:
Five foot tall grass completely obscures the ground and just about anything else
moving upon it. Taller than a pony, a hobbit or even a short man, the thick
grass gets even thicker in the west as it blends into a small grove of elms. All
attempts to peer into the unyielding grass prove fruitless. All even the tallest
elf could see from this elevation are the tops of trees, and blades of grass.
name: Tall Grass

desc:
In the south, grass as tall as a man reaches towards the sky. It sways
gracefully when caught by the occasional breeze. The grass thins out a little
the farther north it grows. It appears that something is pulling the life out of
the soil in that direction. As the grass gets more sparse, no other plant life
fills in the barren earth.
name: Thinning Grass

desc:
A lonely oak-tree stands here, so huge that the base dwells in shadow beneath
the enormous canopy - even on the most sunniest of days. The heavy branches bend
under the weight of the mass of leaves on them, like an old stooping man. Over
the years, its long roots have spread through the underground, so that no other
tree grows nearby; and countless cracks run along its rough, hard, knotted bark,
thoroughly carved by the years and the inclemency of the weather. The men of
these lands call it Wisebark, and they are right, for this is one of the most
ancient trees in Arda, and it has been here for more years than any man, orc, or
even elf can count.
name: Wisebark

desc:
The thick stumps of what once were large oaks dot the plain, their twisting
roots still buried in the earth, as a great cemetery of trees. The oak-wood that
extends to the north once stretched up to here, but now, all that still remains
from those trees are the heavy wooden planks in the nearby buildings, and these
bulky stumps. Long, concentric rings run along their wet, rough, upper surface,
their number giving grave testimony to their great age.
name: A Field of Stumps

desc:
This little long building always seems fresh and in the shade. The light that
comes in through the entrance hole is not enough to illuminate but the first few
steps inside. Everything seems extraordinarily clean. There are large piles of
straw, as golden as the sun, shining out in the dim light. Leaning against the
side wall is a long watering trough, filled with clear, almost crystalline
water. Beside it, lay several brushes and buckets. And despite their obvious
residence here, the smell of the animals is remarkably faint.
name: Beorn's Stables

desc:
The track runs across a group of several wooden buildings of different sizes.
There are granaries, sheds and barns, and a few others whose purposes are not
clear, and they all have walls made of thick, rough, oaken logs, and most of
them are thatched-roofed, but some have wooden roofs instead. A great oak-wood
extended all over this place way back, but a large part of it has been felled,
and only the wide belt that surrounds the farmstead, and a few huge and lonely
ancient trees, that grow scattered inside, are still standing. These few trees
cast ample shadows, in which the animals can lie on the grass to rest.
name: Path through Some Outbuildings

desc:
This high square building is divided into two floors. Supported by stout trunks
that serve as pillars, a several-inches-thick platform rises a few feet over the
floor, forming some sort of mezzanine that takes up most of the inner section. A
heavy ramp made of thick planks ascends along the wall towards there, where
large dark-brown sacks pile up. In the lower floor, many enormous barrels of
reinforced wood are kept in store, together with big pots and jars of dark-red,
rough earth.
name: A Wooden Granary

desc:
Located in front of the entrance to the long, U-shaped wooden house, the sides
of this little courtyard are enclosed by the house wings, as its back side is by
the front wall, where a small and dark door opens into the house. In the middle,
there is a great oak trunk that shows evident signs of axe hacks. A number of
lopped branches pile up in disorder beside it. To the north, a grassy track goes
through the outbuildings, before continuing off into the distance.
name: The Courtyard

desc:
A large hearth occupies the middle of this spacious, long hall, and it remains
lit throughout the whole year, filling the room with the crackling of logs, the
smell of burning wood, and the warmth of its fire. The smoke climbs lazily in
search of the way out through an opening in the ceiling, past the heavy
blackened rafters. Two rows of thick pillars, with what appear to be brackets
for torches, support the roof that, like the walls and the floor, is made of
wood. Slightly raised platforms run along both side walls, that are abutted by
the wings, and so have no windows. Thus, the room is only dimly lit. On the
north, a dark door leads outside, and so does a smaller one in the rear wall.
name: Beorn's Hall

desc:
Running through the wide gardens, this narrow little path heads northwards,
parallel to the western wing of the house. Its outer wall is low and has no
windows, and it is made of large, circular, hardly-worked trunks. Similarly, the
roof ascends steeply towards the peak, right along the middle of the house.
Flowers crowd around the track, and they seem to have been deliberately planted
to grow facing it, offering their colour to whoever follows the way.
name: The Garden Path

desc:
These wide gardens, crammed with colour and liveliness, spread over most of the
southern area of the farmhouse. A great assortment of flowers and plants are
here, and some of them are so extremely rare that they may not be found anywhere
else. Some have big crowns of silky petals; others are small and
handbell-shaped; a few seem long and swaying; the variety is endless. And they
shine in the most variegated colours, in deep and pale shades, and dark and
light hues, like a great rainbow over the ground, but one with its stripes mixed
and intermingled in a myriad of coloured blotches.
name: Flowered Gardens

desc:
Concealed behind the impenetrable veil of the thick, grassy wall, a wide, deep
cavern goes into the gritty rock of the embankment. Even at the entrance light
is extremely dim, as only a few scarce rays manage to get through the grass
hedge. But their tenuous light is enough to make a myriad of tiny glimmering
points shine on the rock surface, and, on the dark, vaulted ceiling, they
resemble the starry sky. Further inside, shadows crowd and darkness becomes
total. Hundreds upon hundreds of deer skeletons lie scattered on the ground, for
this is the secret place where they come to die, and thus they have done for
countless generations.
name: The Red Deer Cemetery

desc:
This extensive porch, propped on wooden posts made of single tree-trunks, faces
south. Big, wooden, comfortable-looking benches allow you to sit and enjoy the
sight of the gardens, especially under the warmth of the westering sun, when the
last golden rays fall slanting on the flowers. A few broad steps descend into
the garden, where a small path seems to detour the house on its western flank,
and then it heads northwards.
name: A Veranda by the Gardens

desc:
This room is rather large but completely cramped with crates, sacks and loose
supplies, ranging from materials for the smith's work to items for everyday use.
Everything is ordered nicely and every crate or item seems to have its place.
Near the southern window lie a lot of tools and items that obviously are used to
fix worn out cloths, leathers, and other tools next to a cushion.
name: A Cramped Storage

desc:
The Old Forest Road comes to an abrupt end at this large wall of brush. During
the day, it is obvious where the wall ends and the gates begin because the
good-natured inhabitants of this town leave the gates open. But at night, when
necessity demands more security and prudence, the gates are closed, and it
becomes virtually impossible to discern the end of the wall from the start of
the gates.
name: At the Gates of Ingrove

desc:
The Old Forest Road makes a deep curve from the north to the east, leaving the
way west partly blocked by a steep earthen wall. The surface of the road
consists mostly of dry mud with here and there small dots of yellowish green
grass. Circular entrances of the nests of swallows are abundant in the steep
earthen wall.
name: Bend in the Old Forest Road

desc:
The field here is covered with grass and flowers, enriching the landscape with
their colours. A small ridge, some ten feet long, creates a shady place where
brown coloured mushrooms, some with teeth marks of small animals, are
prospering. To the north a hollow road leading to some sort of forest is barely
visible between the grasslands.
name: Ridged Grassland

desc:
The northern border of the Old Forest Road is covered by huge trees. The grass
to the south once was evenly abundant on the road, however today the road is
only covered with small patches of dry grass in between large patches of mud.
After a while the trees to the north become so dense that they form an
impenetrable wall. High above, in one of the huge trees a small wooden platform
has been built.
name: Old Forest Road

desc:
Between grassy plains the Old Forest Road, covered with tracks of heavy carts,
runs from east to west. A muddy path, to which most fresh tracks lead, runs
south into the open plains. To the northwest a settlement, protected by dense
trees and a wooden wall, dominates the view. A stone, engraved with some
markings, has been placed at the junction of the path and road.
name: Junction in the Old Forest Road

desc:
Due to centuries of travel the road has been worn out, now forming a hollow way
between the plains. The road itself is partly overgrown, but some imprints of
hoofs are still present. The steep banks bordering it are covered in weeds and
small flowers, with here and there a small hole in them. Further away the flat
plains are covered with grasses and small shrubs.
name: Old Forest Road

desc:
The old hollow road runs through vast grasslands, and is itself partly overgrown
with grasses and weeds. Stones, which used to border the road, are barely
visible between tussocks of grass and piled up dirt. In the grasslands
surrounding the road, some solitary trees and low bushes grow here and there.
name: Old Forest Road

desc:
The grasslands are covered with a thick vegetation of typical grassland plants,
like clover, white daisies, purple thistle and yellow dandelion. Some grey
stones, mostly covered with yellow lichen, placed in a circular pattern form a
relic of an ancient civilisation. To the north of those stones a depression
splits the plains into two parts.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
The grass grows high on the dark black soil, leaving no space for clover or any
other weeds. A few large grey stones covered with lichen dot the grassland. In
the shade of one of these stones small mushrooms grow on the fertile soil. To
the north a road, lying lower than the surrounding plains, runs from east to
west, while to the west a trail leads to the south.
name: Plains

desc:
This trail, covered with fresh tracks of cartwheels, is bordered by some large
willow trees and rotten logs of former trees. A small spring, dotted with animal
tracks, makes the path muddy, but also provides water to the trees and the
surrounding grassland. The trail splits off a larger road in the north and
continues its way to the south.
name: Old Trail

desc:
The grass in this grassland has grown to a height of a few feet, indicating a
rich soil beneath it. At some places the long stalks have toppled due to the
wind and rain. The quiet sounds of small animals rustling through the high grass
and buzzing insects fill the air. To the north the grasslands are interrupted by
a road and a small path intercepts them to the east.
name: Grassland

desc:
The ground is invisible because of the thick layer of vegetation. Grass covers
most of the plains, except where young trees and low bushes have taken over its
role. Some unnatural ditches divide the plains into regular sections. Water has
collected into each of the even ditches giving reeds and other wetland plants a
suitable habitat.
name: Overgrown Fields

desc:
Lush grass and large bushes cover the fertile ground of these plains. A few
scattered trees dot the landscape, the shade from their leaves providing some
protection from the elements to the creatures that live in the area. Signs of
small fauna can be seen across the landscape, the most obvious being an animal
trail leading south toward a copse of birch trees.
name: Fertile Plains

desc:
Close to the path in the west the remains of a camp fire can be seen; a circle
of grey stones surround a patch of burned earth. Around the stones the grasses
covering the plains have been pressed down by something large. A few skeletons
of deer, with knife marks all over them, lie scattered around those flattened
spaces.
name: Abandoned Camp

desc:
The trail, consisting of bare soil with small pebbles, is replaced by rich
grasslands to the east. In this area a dark patch, probably created by a fire,
is visible. Just west of the trail a haystack with a long wooden pole in the
middle has been made by the local people. Beyond the haystack cultivated fields
dominate the view.
name: Old Trail

desc:
Dry grasses, cut from their roots close to the soil, cover the extent of this
hayfield. Scattered among the yellowish grass pyramid-shaped constructions made
of thin wooden stakes have been erected. On these wooden frames fluffy patches
of grass have been spread out which are drying in the wind. To the east a
haystack has been constructed next to a trail, while to the south a small ditch
borders the same trail.
name: Hayfield

desc:
The landscape here has two quite different looks. To the west of a stone wall
pasture fields dominate the view, while east of the wall the rich black soil,
typical of this area, is revealed. On these recently ploughed fields birds look
for easy meal consisting of worms and larvae.
name: Pasture and Fields

desc:
The trail across the grassland is marked with tracks of carts and cattle and
bordered with some moss covered grey stones. One of them appears to have some
markings on it. Some flies hover above a fresh pile of droppings on the path. A
partly overgrown track leading south diverges from the main path leading to some
buildings.
name: Trail near a Village

desc:
The trail curves from north to west through the rich fields before leading to a
small village. Fresh tracks of carts and some droppings at the edge of the trail
indicate recent humanoid use. At the edge of the trail the vestiges of a cart,
wheels broken and the planks rotten, is partly hidden by the vegetation. At the
southern border regular stacked willow branches form a few tall piles.
name: Bend in an Old Trail

desc:
Grasses and other vegetation grow abundantly on the rich soil. The local birds
build their nests in the few solitary trees growing in this area, while animal
tracks leading from small holes beneath the trunks of those trees. The plains
extend to all directions except to the west where they are bordered by a trail.
name: Rich Plains

desc:
A small copse of birch trees surrounds a waterhole that provides sustenance to
fauna in the area; trails made by deer and small mammals converge to this place
from the surrounding plains. This permanent source of water allows for a lush
grass cover that provides smaller mammals with shelter. Several shrubs are
scattered among the trees; laden with berries they make ideal food sources for
birds and other small animals.
name: A Copse of Birch

desc:
On the ground the grass seems healthy; dry ears with spikelets consisting of
some florets raise above the green level of the leaves. The purple flowers of
thistles add colour to the surrounding landscape. Some animal tracks, marked by
droppings in the flattened grass lead to the east where a few birch trees
decorate the horizon.
name: The Purple Plains

desc:
The willow saplings have been planted close together in the fertile soil.
Crowded in such a dense grove, the willows have grown straight and tall with few
side branches at all. Some open spaces in this miniature forest and piles of
neatly stacked branches north near a trail indicate recent harvesting of some of
the year-old saplings. To the south the young trees are replaced by older
knotted ones.
name: Willow Saplings

desc:
A trail worn by the passage of both humans and animals passes through the
plains. Nevertheless, recent tracks are scarce and small shrubs and bushes grow
in different spots along it. The trail runs along a gentle slope from the
harvested fields to the west towards small copse of trees to the east.
name: Old Trail

desc:
The trail, completely covered by long thick grass approximately three feet tall,
bends from a northward to a westward direction, avoiding the marsh to the south.
East of the trail young willow trees, with thin straight branches emerging from
their knotted crowns, grow in unnaturally straight lines.
name: Bend in an Overgrown Trail

desc:
The trees in the southern marshy part of the willow fields are very old, while
to the north on dryer ground the trees seem younger and healthier. Thick crowns,
spanning several feet sit on top of the thick short trunks. From these crowns
thin branches make the top of the trees look like giant hedgehogs. At the shade
side of the willow trunks brown fungi decorate them. To the south and west
wetlands border the forest.
name: Old Willow Fields

desc:
Lush grasses cover these fertile fields which stretch south and east. While
birds and small mammals seem to make up most of the animal life, hoof marks form
the evidence of grazing by large mammal. Occasional ash and birch trees dot
these plains, providing shelter for sparrows and other birds that can be seen
flying about. To the west the grasses are replaced by fields of old willows.
name: Open Grassland

desc:
These rich grasslands stretch west and south, the tall grass carpet broken only
by the occasional birch or elm tree. Shrubs and a small patch of willow trees
surround a small depression filled with muddy water containing small larvae and
bugs. Trails left by large animals feeding on the leaves of these shrubs are
marked by dried droppings and grazed leaves. To the northeast the gloomy eaves
of Mirkwood can be seen in the distance.
name: Depression in the Open Grassland

desc:
Sparse brush covers the terrain here, providing shelter and maintenance to
myriad smaller animals. An overgrown and seldom used trail is all that is left
between the stones bordering once a wide path. Little evidence is left of anyone
using this route at all, save for the remains of a small camp site. However,
northwards and westwards cultivated fields are the sign of recent human activity
in this area.
name: Bend in the Overgrown Trail

desc:
The fields are covered with regular spaced stumps of agriculture products like
corn and wheat, with deep tracks of wheels marking the places where carts stood
to load the harvested crops. On the ground mice run around collecting and eating
the leftovers, while in the sky birds spying for prey circle around. To the
north a low earthen wall covered with shrubs blocks the passage.
name: Harvested Fields

desc:
A weather-beaten trail running north to south can still be clearly seen,
nevertheless time-worn and faded tracks are evidence of little, if any, recent
traffic. To the north, in the distance, a small farm can be distinguished. To
the west some field, the uses of which cannot be determined from this distance,
fill the horizon.
name: Fading Trail

desc:
As the curvy trail continues from north to west, one can see a little path,
probably made by animals leading southwards. The trail, originally covered with
some small pieced gravel, is now mostly overgrown by low grasses and weeds.
Beside the trail an old log, providing a view of the marshy grasslands to the
east, is placed like a bench.
name: Curve in an Overgrown Trail

desc:
Luxuriant grasses cover the extent of this tilted grassland. Scattered among the
grass small woody shrubs with crimson and yellow flowers grow, filling the air
with their scents. In the low southwestern part of this grassland tall cattails,
with staminate flowers rising above the leaves, grow between the grass hinting
at a soggy underground.
name: Wet Grassland

desc:
Man made ditches, filled with a few feet of water, cut through the wetlands,
dividing it into long stretches of floating mats of vegetation. On one of these
mats a small shelter, with a view across a small lake, has been constructed out
of rough wooden poles and braided twigs. Near the trail to the northwest some
small piles of dark brown peat have been placed, ready for transportation.
name: Wetlands

desc:
These fertile plains show a increased number of birch and oak trees as the
terrain slowly rises towards the south into the hills. In addition to the trees
a few small bushes and shrubs provide food and shelter to a variety of small
birds and mammals. To the east the terrain slowly rises to the sky before ending
abruptly at a steeply declining rock face, preventing travel in that direction.
name: Lightly-wooded Plains

desc:
The grasses, richly coloured with flowers, grow abundantly in these fields,
providing a perfect habitat for insects and herbivores like deer and wild sheep.
Some stones, covered with moss, stick out between the grasses covering this
field. Just above this moss, small capsules carrying spores weave in the wind.
To the west the grassland drops significantly showing some small pools.
name: Grassland

desc:
Lush grasses cover these fertile fields which stretch north, east and south.
These grasses are filled with insect life that provides ample sustenance to the
birds that fly overhead. Several trees and shrubs grow, scattered across the
landscape, providing roosts for these birds and hiding places for other small
animals. To the southeast a hill rises, towering over the plains.
name: Open Grassland

desc:
Lush grasses cover these fertile fields and those that stretch west and north.
While the thriving insect life provides ample sustenance to small birds and
mammals, the sparse bushes and shrubs provide little protection from predators
and inclement weather. Southwards a lightly wooded plain leads to some rising
hills.
name: Open Grassland

desc:
The grassland in the east is replaced by wetlands with small pools whose banks
are covered by spike-rush, with tiny flowers at the top, and long reeds. The
wetland itself consists mostly of thick floating mats of slender sedge
characterized by bare stems with very long, very thin leaves. On the thicker
parts of this floating surface some orchids dye the landscape purple.
name: Wetlands

desc:
The soggy nature of these wetlands makes it hard to keep balance. The surface,
consisting of mosses, has a healthy light green look, apart from the places
where the water level has risen above the vegetation. At the places where the
ground is more solid thick patches of grass and some solitary birch and willow
trees grow. To the west, at higher grounds, a trail can be spotted.
name: Wetlands near a Trail

desc:
The Old Forest Road runs through a vast, grassy plain. The broad and well-built
road must have been actively used in the past, but is nowadays partly overgrown
with the wilderness, making the road look somewhat decayed and almost abandoned.
At the outer edge of the road, a steady row of low bushes follow it with an
occasional tree growing here and there among the bushes.
name: Old Forest Road

desc:
The surface of the low grassland is mainly coloured in different shades of light
brown, but green and yellow are also well represented. Half buried in the dry
grass, an animal skeleton is visible. A row of old fence poles sticks in a
straight row between the road and the plain. Farther away at the plain, there
are a few isolated clumps of yew trees with lowgreen bushes growing around them.
name: Low Grasslands

desc:
The grass grows in abundance in this field. The vast plain is filled with
grasses and various kinds of flowers. Around them, bees and other insects hover
in search of nectar. The rich vegetation makes these fields a pleasant habitat
for herbivores as well; fresh deer and rabbit tracks wander aimlessly throughout
the area. South of this sea of life, an old road can be spotted.
name: Grasslands

desc:
The wide grassland is covered with high grasses and lush, dense bushes. The
ground looks quite tangled and messy due to all the wild vegetation. At some
areas, several acres are covered by dark green bushes, while elsewhere it is
fully covered by plants with hollow stems and parallel-veined leaves. Throughout
the plains a few small tree saplings grow.
name: Near the Cleared Forest

desc:
A long time ago, large trees, most notably oaks and beeches, dwelt here. Now
only large stumps spanning more than five feet high remain. Ferns and mosses
grow between the roots of the trunks, among the occasional holes made by small
animals, that can be spotted on the ground. The gaps between the stumps are
inhabited by young willow trees and bramble bushes.
name: Cleared Forest

desc:
The Old Forest Road bends around a small rise to the north-east. A few old cart
tracks imprinted in the dry mud form the surface of the road. At the southern
boundary of the road, rocks and stones of all sizes have carefully been aligned
with each other to prevent the erosion of the road in the case of heavy rains.
Thin grass tufts grow from the cracks of the stones.
name: Old Forest Road

desc:
This clear stretch of the Old Forest Road runs directly from the east to the
west. The sparse vegetation close to the road, in combination with the thick
bushes further away show that the road has been wider in the past. To the north
flat plains can be seen, whilst to the south a thick thorny hedge planted on top
of a low dike blocks all passage.
name: Old Forest Road

desc:
The Old Forest Road runs from the east to the west. In most places, the surface
of the road is hard and dusty. In the middle of the road, faint traces of
cartwheels can be spotted among the other bumps and holes of the road, while at
the edges, grasses and clovers are in the process of retaking the road. On the
south side of the road, a low dike covered with bushes blocks the view.
name: Old Forest Road

desc:
The sandy, light brown road makes a long, steady curve, as it winds from the
west to the south. The road is several yards in width, allowing even two wagons
to easily pass each other. There are low, grassy trenches on either side of the
road. Behind those, the ground elevates quickly a few yards, giving the
impression of travelling in a dry riverbed. Thick trees grow on the eastern side
of the road, making both the travel and visibility very limited.
name: Winding Old Forest Road

desc:
These fields have clearly been used for farming in the past, but since then they
have become quite feral. The ground, partly covered with young bushes and high
grass, is uneven. Several long rows of bumpy furrows are still visible within
the undergrowth. To the north, the fields are bordering the Old Forest Road.
Apart from that, the flat fields continue on in all directions.
name: Abandoned Fields

desc:
The field is covered with different types of vegetation. A short yellowish
grass, with several shades of brown and green, fills the ground. Low bushes and
occasional flower clumps grow here and there. Few beech trees stand above the
other foliage, giving some protection from the sun. Open plains seem to continue
in every direction, although to the south the ground begins to slope.
name: Wild Fields

desc:
A thin layer of soil covers the barren, rocky ground. While some spots are
totally bare, other places are covered by grass and dark green bushes. Here and
there are nasty, rough rocks sticking up from the ground, making travelling
quite slow and unpleasant with their sharp edges. Underneath the sparse bushes,
tracks of small animals criss-cross the soil before disappearing into the small
holes between the rocks.
name: Uneven Plains

desc:
Half of the plain is completely covered by bushes and dense vegetation, whereas
the other part has been charcoaled by a devastating fire. Shrubs and bushes to
the east have been eradicated entirely, and only a few large rocks and stones
stick up from the burnt ground. An extremely steep slope leads to a bare valley
to the south. There are no visible paths around, which makes the movement
difficult. Behind the valley, magnificent hills can be seen.
name: Charred Plains

desc:
Short, yellow-green grass grows on the bottom of the valley with several rocks
and spiny bushes covering the ground. Steep valley walls rise abruptly, and
cliffs on the northern wall are especially filled with loose rocks and boulders
which make it too dangerous to proceed in that direction. A narrow but flat
valley floor slithers among the rocks and continues in every direction but
north. A majestic hill stands proudly in the south.
name: Dangerous Valley

desc:
This small, steep valley lies between the high hills and vast plains. The ground
is rocky and quite barren, offering easy access in all directions but the north,
where the valley wall is too dangerous to travel in. A fire recently spread
through the northern plains, carried by a wind and dumping ash, covering parts
of the valley with death-like greyness.
name: Steep Valley

desc:
The slope is growing with thick, but relatively short, green grass. The incline
starts from a broad valley a few miles to the north, and then elevates steadily
throughout the whole region, towards the steep slope of an enormous hill in the
south. To the west, the ground descends abruptly into a rocky valley, allowing
no access. A small path winds down from the hill, turning around some grey
boulders, and leading to a junction which is visible from the east.
name: Sloping Plains

desc:
A rocky valley is resting at the foot of a majestic hill. The steep edges of the
valley rise upwards towards the sky as the end of the valley gets closer. The
valley ends at a round clearing, which is surrounded by a sheer rock face from
all directions. Brown rocks form an impassable hillside, covered with small pits
and black narrow hollows. Far above, few bushy trees have managed to grow on the
vertical surface.
name: Rocky Valley

desc:
Grey rocks and withered bushes are scattered over this rather flat plain. The
western side of the plain has a slightly higher elevation, where the terrain is
slowly rising towards some high hills that are visible to the southwest. Several
paths meet each other here, forming a small junction. A huge rock rests solidly
in the middle of it, with three arrows carved upon it. There are no signs of
life anywhere, except for a few old fireplaces along the path left by
travellers.
name: Meeting of the Paths

desc:
Dense foliage and a thick growth of low shrubs covers the plain, forming a wild
mixture of both deciduous and evergreen plants. The vegetation opens up just
enough for a wagon-wide path to clear through the field. The western parts of
the plain are more rocky, and a steep valley prevents those who pull themselves
through the bushes to pass by any further.
name: Bushy Plains

desc:
A fire has burned down most of the vegetation, only grey ash and charred, black
branches remain. A path leads through the fields, it is not very wide, and at
some regions it is hard to find under the windswept shrubs and ash. The land to
the north has been untouched by the fire, and has the look of cultivated
farmland. The eastern terrain is filled with sharp rocks and charred bushes,
making it impossible to travel there.
name: Path through the Charred Plains

desc:
The plain has been completely consumed by a raging fire. As the flames died out,
they left behind a few charred bushes, sooty stones, and a large amount of dark
grey ash. The landscape is otherwise flat, except for to the south where the
charcoaled plain transforms into a steep, dangerous-looking slope which descends
abruptly into the rocky valley below.
name: Charred Bushes

desc:
The grass grows sumptuously on the field, which contains the old remains of
beets, carrots, and potatoes. The field is divided by an ancient, weathered
fence made up of old, grey poles and rotten planks. The majority of the planks
have come lose from the poles, and now lie rotting under the grass. A few old
reinforced mangers, made of thick oaken beams, are still standing on the fields.
name: Abandoned Fields

desc:
Several square-shaped open areas have been cleared on the otherwise bushy
plains. Squares look cultivated, and are growing vegetables and other plants.
Regularly used grassy paths surround the fields. At the lower places a faint
smell of excrement, possibly manure, comes from the ground. At the end of the
fields are piles of leaves and rotten vegetables. A long, trimmed hedge
surrounds a small farm area located to the east.
name: Farmed Fields

desc:
This field is covered with beets. They have green leaves with red veins, and in
many cases the top part of the red root has been transformed to a slight green
by the light of the sun. At the northern end of the field, a broad dike covered
with dense bushes protects the fields from cold winds and from the unwanted
trespassers travelling the Old Forest Road.
name: Field covered with Beets

desc:
A small, dusty path, covered with old footprints, courses between the fields.
The western field has been retaken by high grasses, bushes, and other plantlife.
Here and there, a pole from a former fence is visible in the middle of this
feral wilderness. A small ditch separates the eastern wheat fields from the
path. A wide track of fallen wheat is visible on the field, it looks as if some
big animal has wandered through the crop.
name: Path through the Fields

desc:
A narrow trail separates from the dusty path, leading into the front yard of a
farmhouse made of thick, wooden planks. The outside of this house was once
painted in bright red and orange, but due to the sun's bleaching, the colours
have faded into a shade of light brown, with the exception of right below the
roof where the original splendour is still visible. A long, thick hedge
circulates the fields behind the house.
name: Bend in the Dusty Path

desc:
A major part of this farmed soil has been planted with potatoes, which are
growing well with green leaves and white flowers. Everywhere else, rotten
potatoes can be seen breaking out of the soil, ravaged by critters. A tall dike,
topped by several thick bushes, blocks the passage to the north and east. A
small natural passage has formed through the wall to the north, due to erosion.
name: Potato Fields

desc:
The Old Forest Road is covered with dirt and short grass. It curves around a
small hill located north-east of the road. The hill is mainly covered with low
bushes and a few solitary tree that rise into the sky. On the west side of the
road, the passage is blocked by a long dike covered with bramble bushes. In the
south, a dirt path lined with trees leads to some buildings.
name: Winding Old Forest Road

desc:
The brown plain is covered with bushes. A small path runs through the terrain,
almost completely hidden by all the thorns, leaves, and branches. At the
southern end of the plain, a path travels through a minor dip. Dense bushes are
blocking the way to the east and south, and the bushy plains continue onwards to
the northwest.
name: Path through the Bushes

desc:
Thick thorn bushes surround a small, windy path that is partially visible from
all the dead branches that cover it. Something seems to be rustling among the
surrounding thorns, making the vegetation move. The northern and western parts
of the field are completely covered with sharp thorns, allowing travellers to
only move to the east and south.
name: Bushy Plains

desc:
The scrubland is a tangled mess of feral bushes and small trees, which have
grown together with long, dry weeds. Parts of the shrubbery have formed thickets
so dense that the slithering old path heading south has become half-buried under
the vegetation. Thorny shrubs seem to continue on all directions, making
navigation through these plains restricted.
name: Path through the Tangled Bushes

desc:
With no other way out of the deep vegetation than to the north, the path ends at
a stony clearing within the shrubbery. The dense thicket encircling the path
makes it impossible to penetrate through, or view beyond, towards the
surrounding area. This seems like a good place for travellers to settle down for
the night, as it provides shelter against the strong wind that often crosses
these plains. Several deep tracks can be seen in the grass, maybe left by mounts
and wagons of other travellers.
name: End of the Path

desc:
Shrivelled grasses, bushes, and small plants are located all over this flat
plain. The terrain has been under drought, keeping the plants stunted. A few
noises that can be heard are coming from the small insects that fly by. The
rocky ground to the north and west hinders travel there, while the plain
stretches out elsewhere seemingly endless.
name: Withered Plains

desc:
This infertile and desolate plain extends a great distance eastwards, allowing
the wind to blow harshly through the area. Stones of various sizes and types lie
on the ground, especially to the north, where the plain seems impenetrable due
to the sharp rocks and stones. The plains are riddled with cracks among the
short grass, where the mud has hardened and split due exposure from the
elements.
name: Withered Plains

desc:
This bleak plain is barren, containing only some modest vegetation, as even the
most adaptable flora has difficult time trying to gain a foothold in this dry
terrain. Some sandy brown and light green coloured, dried bushes still exist,
growing among the stunted grass tufts. The soil is withered as far as the eye
can see, and cracked in several locations.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
The terrain is a verdant grasslands, with a few scattered clumps of trees and
bushes growing here and there. Rising from the lush grass, a long, rocky
escarpment dominates the area to the east, creating an impassable stony wall in
the otherwise featureless terrain. A few boulders are planted at the foot of the
rock wall, which looks so worn that the new boulders may fall down at any
moment.
name: Rocky Escarpment

desc:
A grassy, green path runs through the winding fields, connecting the Old Forest
Road from the north to a small farm located in the west. A neat line of beech
and ash trees has been planted on both sides of the path at regular intervals.
The slender and delicate trees are about four or five feet tall.
name: Grassy Path

desc:
The road is covered with loose sand, with an occasional small tussock here and
there. It is hard to distinguish the boundary between the road and the
surrounding plains, because the grass gradually increases in height and in
density. The northern edge of the road is interrupted by a small path running
north towards a hut. To the east, the border of the forest consisting of huge
trees can be seen.
name: Old Forest Road

desc:
A low roofed hut stands at the forest border. There is a modest yard around the
house, surrounded by a rough, bright yellow plank fence. Huge piles of wood have
been placed between the nearest trunks of the forest. A thicket of dense
branched trees and messy underbrush starts behind the fence. A small path starts
from the garden gate and forks, where the path to the south joins the Old Forest
Road opposite the eastern path leads into a dark forest.
name: Small Path by the Forest Border

desc:
The walls and the floor of this small hut have been constructed of thick wooden
beams, while the roof is roughly built of planks and bricks. There is a small
window next to the entrance, giving a view to the garden outside. Axes, saws and
wedges hang from nails on the wall, along with some worn working clothes. Close
to the back wall, a cupboard divides the hut in such a way that a confined space
containing a bed has been created to the far end of the room.
name: Lumberjack's Hut

desc:
A worn out path leads through the dim forest, winding slowly between the trees
and bushes. Someone has been chopping a lot of trees down recently, as stumps,
cut branches, and other trash from the fallen trees litter the forest; even a
few small clearings have been formed next to the path. Outside the cleared parts
the tall, shady trees are covered with hanging vines and other entangled
vegetation. The forest looks dark and dangerous.
name: Forest Path

desc:
The shady forest is dark and extremely threatening. It is unknown how high the
ancient trees reach, as the unbroken net of vines, leaves, and branches form the
ceiling a few yards above the ground. Nothing seems to be moving anywhere, even
the air is totally still. This place has a strong malignant aura; it is as if
the gloomy forest was alive and an ancient evilness was creeping behind every
bush and tree.
name: Murky Forest

desc:
Huge, bumpy trees grow here, forming impenetrable clumps in the dense forest.
The colour of the trunks varies between green, brown, and black among the
occasional fungi that grows upon some trunks. There is quite a lot of
miscellaneous ground-level foliage, but not so much that it would block the
passage. The uppermost branches tangle together forming an almost impenetrable
canopy.
name: Dense Forest

desc:
The eastern edge of the forest is filled with young, vital trees, thriving
clusters of thorn bushes, and small, open areas within the forest, where the
mushrooms and ferns grow. Looking deeper into the woods, the trees are
significantly taller and thicker, and the vegetation gets quickly denser. Here
and there are closely set copses, where brambles grow around the thick tree
formations.
name: Border of the Forest

desc:
There is a wide, flat clearing occupying the area. It grows hay, low bushes and
other undergrowth allowing clear visibility into all directions. The surrounding
trees slowly continue their attempts to reclaim the earth, having began their
journey many years ago. Someday the looming forest walls on both sides will meet
here again, as they did in the ancient times when Mirkwood was still known as
Greenwood the Great and the shadow had not fallen on the peaceful forest.
name: Wide Clearing

desc:
The spacious barn looks very clean, as if someone has been taking excellent care
of it. All of the supplies and items are in good condition and well- organised.
